docs.title: Skinnervation3D Analysis Tasks
docs.text:
This package provides a collection of Fractal-compatible tasks to analyse the skin innervation from 3D image data acquired with a mesoSPIM microscope. See the project website for more information on the pre-processing steps of the mesoSPIM acquisitions.

This package currently offers tasks for:

  • Modelling the DEJ surface (Fit Surface)
  • Segmenting fibers (Segment Fibers)
  • Counting the number of fiber crossings (Count Number of Fiber Crossing)
  • Computing the fiber density per structure (Compute Fiber Density per Structure)
  • Analyse fiber network (Analyse Fiber Network)

These tasks are compatible with the Fractal Analytics Platform, where they can be integrated into scalable workflows and executed efficiently on a cluster environment. However, all tasks can also be executed locally and individually on a single machine using standard shell commands or in the SkInnervation3d App, without requiring a full Fractal deployment. Instructions for running tasks locally are provided in the Usage section.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
You can install the package either directly into a Conda/Mamba environment or in a Python virtual environment.

Note

If you plan on using the package on a Fractal plateform, you don't need to install it locally and to follow the procedure below.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: The package requires python version>=3.11, <3.13.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
It is strongly recommended to install this package inside an isolated environment to avoid dependency conflicts. You may use either Conda/Mamba or a Python virtual environment. After the environment is created, the package is installed using pip.

  1. Create a Conda or Mamba environment (recommended)

    An environment.yml file is provided to ensure all dependencies are installed with compatible versions. Additionally a environment_dev.yml is provided to install the package in development mode with dev dependencies.

    With Mamba/Conda: 

    mamba env create -f environment.yml  
mamba activate skin3d-tasks

    For Conda/Mamba installation, refer to the Conda documentation and Mamba documentation.

  2. Install the package in a Python virtual environment

    python -m venv venv  
source venv/bin/activate  
pip install skinnervation3d-fractal-tasks

Note

The package is not yet available on PyPI.

docs.title: How To Crop ROIs
docs.text: This page describes how to efficiently crop ROIs from the OME-Zarr image so that they comply with the expectations of the Fit Surface task. In particular, for the surface modelling algorithm to work properly, the surface, a.k.a. the DEJ, needs to be perpendicular to one of the 3 axes and fully contained within the ROI. This means that it should run from on the top to the bottom of the image and/or from left to right of the image on two different axes. In this section, we describe how to crop the ROIs to ensure this is met.

docs.title: Cropping ROIs Guide
docs.text:
1. First, open the Napari plugin Crop Tool. Consult the User Guide of the plugin to learn how to use the plugin.

  2. To ensure that the surface is perpendicular to an axis, try to switch the scrolling axis until the DEJ appears horizontal or vertical in the viewed 2D plane.

  3. Once the DEJ appears horizontal or vertical in the viewer, you can draw a rectangle representing the ROI section and set the start and end coordinates of the ROI for the scrolling axis. To draw the ROI, first select the Cropping box in the Layer section of the Napari viewer. Then, select the Rectangle Shape in the layer controls. You can now start drawing the rectangle where you want to crop the image:

    You can modify the size of the rectangle by dragging the corners. To do so, you need to click on Select in the layer controls:

  4. To ensure the DEJ is perpendicular to an axis, set the start coordinate when the DEJ is at the bottom or at the left of the rectangle:

  5. Then move the scrolling axis until it reaches the top or right of the rectangle to define the other end of the scrolling axis:

  6. You have now defined the bounding cube of the ROI! You can continue to crop another ROI by repeating the steps above. The start a new ROI, simply draw a new rectangle:

    If you need to move around the image to center it where you need to crop the next ROI, you can do so by clicking on the Move camera in the layer controls of the Napari viewer.

docs.title: Analyse Fiber Network
docs.text:
This task uses the previously segmented fibers to analyse their network. To do so, it will skeletonise the fibers and build a graph representation of the fiber skeleton. This consists in a set of nodes and edges where nodes represent branching or end point of the fibers, and edges represent the branches. It takes as input a multi-channel OME-Zarr image containing the labels fibers (see Segment Fibers for more information about the fiber segmentation process). It will output the following new label in the OME-Zarr image:

  • connection map (labeled locations where fibers cross the DEJ)

as well as the following two files to save the computed graph representation of the network:

  • fiber_network_graph.gexf (graph representation in the GEXF format, to view in a graph viewer like Gephi)
  • fiber_network_graph.pkl (graph representation in the pickle format, to ensure full reproducibility)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are mandatory for the task to run and need to be set, others are optional. The optional parameters are mainly meant for troubleshooting or finetuning, when the default analysis leads to undesired results.

docs.title: Angle Threshold (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the angle between two disconnected fibers is deemed acceptable to connect them.

Why it matters

During the graph extraction, the task will use the computed graph to try to connect remaining fiber fragments that seems to belong to the same fiber. To determine if two fiber fragments can be connected, the task will compute the angle between their closest endpoints. If the angle is below a certain threshold and the distance between the two fibers is below a certain threshold, they will be connected.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • The range of valid values are 0 (no connection) to 180.

In the example below, we see how fibers have been connected using the minimum cost path algorithm:

docs.title: Fiber distance (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the maximum distance that can separate two fiber fragments to be connected. Fibers further away than this distance will not be connected.

Why it matters

During the graph extraction, the task will use the computed graph to try to connect remaining fiber fragments that seems to belong to the same fiber. To determine if two fiber fragments can be connected, the task will compute the angle between their closest endpoints. If the angle is below a certain threshold and the distance between the two fibers is below a certain threshold, they will be connected.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • The distance is measured in um and the scale is also in um in Napari. It is thus possible to estimate the distance between two fiber fragments by measuring the distance between their endpoints in the Napari viewer.

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_analyse_network.py

docs.title: Compute Fiber Density per Biological Structure
docs.text:
This task uses the previously segmented fibers to compute the fiber fraction volume of a biological structure such as large vessel, skin muscle or skin gland. It takes as input a multi-channel OME-Zarr image containing the label fibers (output of the task Segment Fiber). It will output new labels in the OME-Zarr image that are:

  • structure mask (biological structure segmented from the image)
  • labeled fibers (fibers with color coded label per separate fiber)
  • selected fibers (only fibers that are connected to the biological structure)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are mandatory for the task to run and need to be set, others are optional. The optional parameters are mainly meant for troubleshooting or finetuning, when the default analysis leads to undesired results.

docs.title: Structure thresholds (mandatory)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the thresholds to use for the watershed segmentation. The watershed segmentation will use a combination of a gradient map that highlights big changes in intensity, e.g. when the structure stands out from the background, and a label map that informs the algorithm what part of the image belongs to what (the structure or the background). The gradient map is used to set a boundary between labels. A given label will be propagated until it reaches a boundary. For more information: watershed segmentation.

Why it matters

The percentiles are used to inform the algorithm what part of the image belongs to what. The higher percentile will label intensities above that range as belonging to the structure. The lower percentile will label intensities below that range as belonging to the background. Depending on the intensity distribution of the image, the percentiles can be adjusted to "catch" the intensity range of the structure.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default values are sufficient
  • The range of valid values are 0 to 100.
  • If you see that the structure is very bright compared to the background, try to increase the upper percentile.
  • If you see that the structure has similar intensity as the background, the difference between the two percentiles can be quite tight. It might need several run to find the right thresholds.

docs.title: Structure Label (mandatory)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the label of the channel to use to detect the structure (e.g. PGP9.5, Lectin...).

Why it matters

The algorithm will use the intensity distribution of the structure channel to segment the structure. It is best to select the channel where the structure stands out the most from the background.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient, e.g. Lectin for vessels and glands, PGP9.5 for muscles.
  • If you see that the structure is very bright in another channel or have poor results with the default channel, try to change the channel.

docs.title: Structure type (mandatory)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the type of structure to segment. It can be either gland, vessel or muscle.

docs.title: Cost Threshold (finetuning)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the path between two disconnected fibers is deemed acceptable to connect them.

Why it matters

The algorithm attemps to connect close enough fibers by using the background-corrected PGP9.5 signal. The higher the signal, the lower the cost. The final cost of the optimal path between two fibers is the sum of the inverse of the intensities along the path. Thus, by setting a low cost threshold, only fibers that have a path between them of high PGP9.5 intensities will be connected. This algorithm allows to still detect fiber segments of low intensities which could not be segmented due to a noisy background during the segmentation task.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • The range of valid values are 0 (no connection) to 10.
  • If you see evident path between previously segmented fibers and the background noise is not too strong, try to increase the cost threshold by 0.5.

docs.title: Fiber distance (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the maximum distance that can separate two fiber fragments to be connected. Fibers further away than this distance will not be connected.

Why it matters

The algorithm attemps to connect close enough fibers by using the background-corrected PGP9.5 signal. It will only consider fibers that are close enough to be connected, thus setting a maximum distance between them minimises the risk of wrongly connecting fibers but also reduces the computational cost of the algorithm.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If there are a lot of fiber segments (>400) that have been segmented, decrease the maximum distance to reduce the computational cost of the algorithm.
  • If you want to skip the fiber connection step, you can set the cost threshold parameter to 0.
  • If there are clear paths between segmented fiber fragments (in the PGP9.5 channel) that have been missed by the segmentation algorithm, try to increase the maximum distance to connect them.

docs.title: Masking ROI (optional)
docs.text:
What it does It tells the algorithm whether to look for a CSV table in the OME-Zarr image group. This table would contain the coordinates of a mask that will hide various parts of the image so as to segment better the structure of interest.

How to build the ROI mask The ROI masks can be built using the napari plugin Crop Tool. Simply draw ROIs on areas that should NOT be segmented as the structure of interest. In particular, these ROI masks are useful when the structure of interest does not have a clear contrast and/or there are other elements of similar contrast in the image. There can be as many ROI masks as you want.

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_compute_fiber_density.py

docs.title: Count Number of Fiber Crossing
docs.text:
This task uses the previously computed surface model of the Dermis-Epidermis Junction (DEJ) and segmentation of the fibers to count the number of fibers that cross the DEJ. It takes as input a multi-channel OME-Zarr image containing the labels surface and fibers (output of task Fit Surface and Segment Fibers). It will output new labels in the OME-Zarr image that are:

  • crossings (labeled locations where fibers cross the DEJ)
  • oriented fibers (fibers with color coded orientation: red=epidermal, grey=dermal)
  • labeled fibers (fibers with color coded label per separate fiber)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are mandatory for the task to run and need to be set, others are optional. The optional parameters are mainly meant for troubleshooting or finetuning, when the default analysis leads to undesired results.

docs.title: Cost Threshold (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the path between two disconnected fibers is deemed acceptable to connect them.

Why it matters

The algorithm attemps to connect close enough fibers by using the background-corrected PGP9.5 signal. The higher the signal, the lower the cost. The final cost of the optimal path between two fibers is the sum of the inverse of the intensities along the path. Thus, by setting a low cost threshold, only fibers that have a path between them of high PGP9.5 intensities will be connected. This algorithm allows to still detect fiber segments of low intensities which could not be segmented due to a noisy background during the segmentation task.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • The range of valid values are 0 (no connection) to 5.
  • If you see evident path between previously segmented fibers and the background noise is not too strong, try to increase the cost threshold by 0.5.

In the example below, we see how fibers have been connected using the minimum cost path algorithm:

docs.title: Fiber distance (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter sets the maximum distance that can separate two fiber fragments to be connected. Fibers further away than this distance will not be connected.

Why it matters

The algorithm attemps to connect close enough fibers by using the background-corrected PGP9.5 signal. It will only consider fibers that are close enough to be connected, thus setting a maximum distance between them minimises the risk of wrongly connecting fibers but also reduces the computational cost of the algorithm.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If there are a lot of fiber segments (>400) that have been segmented, decrease the maximum distance to reduce the computational cost of the algorithm.
  • If there are clear paths between segmented fiber fragments (in the PGP9.5 channel) that have been missed by the segmentation algorithm, try to increase the maximum distance to connect them.

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_count_crossings.py

docs.title: Export Results to CSV
docs.text: This task exports the results computed by the other tasks to a CSV file which can be opened by Excel or other spreadsheet programs. It parses the OME-Zarr metadata to find numerical results such as the fiber density, the number of fibers crossing the DEJ, the surface area, etc.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are mandatory for the task to run and need to be set, others are optional.

docs.title: All Images (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls which image to export results for. If True, it will export results for all images in the OME-Zarr. For example, if there are several ROIs in the OME-Zarr, this parameter can be used to export results for all the ROIs at once. By default, it is set to False and thus only the results for the current image will be exported.

docs.title: CSV file (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter allows you to give the path to a specific and existing CSV file to export results to.

Why it matters

The default behaviour is to create a CSV file in the OME-Zarr under the name "results_.csv" in case of a specific image, or "results_all.csv" in case of all images. By giving the path to a specific CSV file, you can directly append new results to an existing CSV file.

docs.title: Source Filename (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter allows you to give the name of the source file used to generate the image. This is in case the source file was not included in the OME-Zarr metadata, which can happen if the image was generated using an older versio of the MesoSPIM Fractal tasks package.

docs.title: Overwrite (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls whether to overwrite existing results in the CSV file. If False, it will raise an error if the specific image entry you want to save in the CSV file already exists.

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_export_results.py

docs.title: Fit Surface
docs.text: This task fits a surface to the Dermis-Epidermis Junction (DEJ) revealed by the Lectin fluorescence. It takes as input a multi-channel OME-Zarr image containing the Lectin fluorescence and outputs the surface modelling the DEJ as a label in the same OME-Zarr image.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are mandatory for the task to run and need to be set, others are optional. The optional parameters are mainly meant for troubleshooting or edge cases, when the default analysis leads to undesired results.

docs.title: Surface axis (mandatory)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter tells the algorithm the orientation of the surface. The algorithm assumes that the Dermis–Epidermis junction:

  • is continuous
  • can be described as one surface
  • is perpendicular to one main axis (Z, Y or X)

With this parameter, we specify which axis is perpendicular to the surface.

Available options

  • z → surface varies along z
  • y → surface varies along y
  • x → surface varies along x

How to choose

The question to answer is:

“If I scroll through the image, along which axis does the surface move up and down and is continuous (e.g. runs from one side to the other) along the other two axes?”

That axis is the correct choice.

Here is an example of an image with a surface perpendicular to the Z axis. This illustrates how to read the axes in Napari.

In Napari, the default layout of a 3D volume is Z, Y, X; with the numbering 0~Z, 1~Y, 2~X. In a 2D view mode, the scrolling axis number is displayed in the lower left corner:

It is possible to make the orientation of the axes visible in the "View" menu, under "Axes":

In the example below, we see a 3D view of the image with the axes displayed; the surface is perpendicular to the 0 axis, which is the Z axis:

docs.title: Border width (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the width (in um) of the border of the image that is taken to estimate the starting positions of the optimal path in the cost map. Here is an example of such border:

Why it matters

The surface modelling algorithm tries to find the optimal path from one side to the other of a 2D section. The only requirement is to set the start and end point of the path. Currently, the code automatically selects the highest local maximum of intensities at the border of the image. In case of noise - either in the no-sample region or due to high-intensity vessels - the starting positions can be wrongly set. Taking the mean intensity of a wider border can reduce the impact of such interferences. The surface is assumed to have a continuous high intensity through the 2D section; noise and vessels are more likely to be only localised.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If the results are not satisfactory (wrong starting positions due to other high-intensity elements), try to increase the border size by 10. Keep increasing the border size until the optimal path is found.

Here is an example of a wrong starting position of the optimal path due to high intensity noise:

💡 Tip This parameter is especially useful when the surface seems to start at a location that has a high intensity due to noise or other high-intensity elements. See also the parameter Background percentile (troubleshooting) and Gradient factor (troubleshooting).

docs.title: Background percentile (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the background is set. Image intensities below this threshold are removed from the image before the surface modelling to avoid taking into account background noise.

Why it matters

When the threshold is too low and signal remains in the background, the algorithm can fail to find the starting position of the optimal path as it searches for the first local maxima from the outside of the sample towards the inside. On the other hand, if the threshold is set too high and the lectin signal is not strong, it can remove too much information and the surface is not modelled correctly.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If the results are not satisfactory (wrong starting positions of the optimal path at the beginning of the 3D volume when scrolling), try to increase the threshold by 5 or 10
  • If the surface is not modelled correctly, especially in the middle of the image and where the lectin intensity is weak, not at the borders, try to decrease the threshold by 5 or 10

Here is an example of a wrong starting position due to a too low background threshold:

And after increasing the background threshold to 80:

💡 Tip This parameter is especially useful when the surface seems to start at a location that has no sample. See also the parameter Border width (troubleshooting) and Gradient factor (troubleshooting).

docs.title: Gradient Factor (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the strength of a corrective field, that is gradually decreasing from the exterior of the sample toward its interior.

In practice, the image is multiplied by this decreasing field so that: - structures close to the border of the sample keep a higher intensity - structures deeper inside the sample have a reduced intensity

This helps reduce the influence of bright structures inside the tissue, such as vessels, that are close to the Dermis–Epidermis junction and could lead to a wrong surface model.

Why it matters

The surface detection algorithm searches for the optimal path as the path along which the intensity is maximal.

If internal structures (for example vessels) are very bright and close to the DEJ, they can attract the optimal path and pull the surface away from the true junction.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the gradient factor is not needed
  • If the detected surface is attracted toward bright internal structures (e.g. vessels), try first setting the decreasing gradient factor to 1, and then increase by some units it until the surface is corrected.

Here is an example where internal high-intensity structures attract the surface due to an insufficient decreasing gradient:

💡 Tip This parameter is especially useful when vessels or other bright features are close to the DEJ and interfere with surface detection.

docs.title: Invert Surface (troubleshooting)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter allows to take control of the orientation of the surface, whether the sample is oriented from top to bottom or bottom to top.

Why it matters

The code automatically tries to detect the orientation of the surface. However, if the surface is not oriented correctly, it can lead to wrong results. This parameter allows to take control and bypass the automatic orientation detection. The algorithm expects the surface to be oriented such as the background is at the top or left of the image, and the sample is at the bottom or right.

How to choose

  • In most cases, it is not needed to be set
  • If the surface modelling is completely aberrant, try to manually set the orientation of the surface by setting this parameter to True or False depending on the actual case of the currently analysed image.

docs.title: Analysis Resolution Level (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls which resolution of the OME-Zarr multi-resolution pyramid is used to detect the Dermis–Epidermis junction.

OME-Zarr images are stored as a multi-resolution pyramid:

  • level 0 → full resolution (largest, most detailed)
  • higher levels → downsampled versions (smaller, faster)

Why it matters

Using a lower resolution:

  • uses less memory
  • runs faster
  • is usually sufficient and even better due to intensity averaging to detect the surface shape

Using a very high resolution:

  • increases RAM usage
  • may cause the task to fail on large datasets

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If your image is too small (less than 1G) → keep the default value
  • If your image is large or your computer runs out of memory → increase the resolution level

Typical values

Image size Suggested level Small volume (<1Gb) 0-2 Medium volume (1-5Gb) 3-4 Large volume (>5Gb) >4

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_fit_surface.py

docs.title: Segment Fibers
docs.text: This task segments fibers revealed by the PGP9.5 fluorescence. It takes as input a multi-channel OME-Zarr image containing the PGP9.5 channel and outputs the fiber mask as a label in the same OME-Zarr image.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: In this section, we describe the parameters of the task. Some parameters are recommended to be set and finetuned per run, others are optional. The optional parameters are mainly meant for troubleshooting or finetuning, when the default analysis leads to undesired results.

docs.title: Background Percentile (recommended)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the background is set. Image intensities below this threshold are removed from the image to reduce background noise.

Why it matters

For this task, the background threshold is calculated locally for each pixel, hence it is more meant to enhance the contrast of the fiber signal rather than removing global background noise. Regarding the global background, it is better to finetune the Fiber Percentile parameter to ensure only fibers are segmented out.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If the results are not satisfactory (fibers too thick, not well defined), try to increase the threshold by 10

Here is an example of a too big background surrounding the fibers:

💡 Tip This parameter is tightly linked to the Fiber Percentile parameter. Finetuning the two parameters can lead to better segmentation of fibers.

docs.title: Fiber Percentile (recommended)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the threshold at which the fibers are segmented. Image intensities above this threshold are selected as fibers.

How to choose

Depending on the strength of the PGP9.5 signal and the amount of the background noise, this parameter needs to be adjusted depending on the current case. The full range of 50-100 can be explored to find the best value.

  • In most cases, high values are preferred (>90) to ensure only fibers are segmented out.
  • The range of valid values is 50-100.
  • Small contrast between fibers and background -> increase fiber threshold
  • High contrast -> decrease fiber threshold to catch also fainter fibers

💡 Tip This parameter is tightly linked to the Background Percentile parameter. Finetuning the two parameters can lead to better segmentation of fibers.

docs.title: Min Fiber Size (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the minimum volume size of the fibers. It is expressed in um3. Segmented elements that have a volume smaller than this threshold are removed.

Why it matters

It can be useful to remove small bright artifacts that are not fibers but of similar intensities.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If there is a lot of bright artifacts, it can be useful to increase this value

docs.title: Max Fiber Size (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the maximum volume size of the fibers. It is expressed in um3. Segmented elements that have a volume bigger than this threshold are removed.

Why it matters

Depending on the background noise, there can be a large volume of high intensity that is segmented alongside the fibers that comes from the epidermis or other structures. Rather than increasing the fiber threshold, it can be useful to simply set a maximum volume size of the fibers to remove such elements as they are likely to be bigger than the fibers.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If a large volume of high intensity is segmented alongside the fibers, try to decrease this value to remove it
  • In case a large fiber network is clearly absent from the fiber mask, try to increase this value to avoid wrongly removing the fibers

docs.title: Correct Halo (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls whether to correct or not for fiber halo in the Z direction. The fiber halo is the bright signal halo that can surround the fibers due to imaging.

Why it matters

If the fibers have a very high signal, it can be difficult to segment out the halo which leads to thick fibers, and sometimes even wrongly connect fibers across Z that are not normally related.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the halo correction is not needed and the default value is sufficient
  • If the fibers are too thick, try to set this parameter to True

docs.title: Lectin Factor (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls the strength of the multiplicative factor that is applied to the Lectin channel before it is subtracted to the PGP9.5 channel. This is done to remove background intensity.

Why it matters

The PGP9.5 channel is often affected by the sample background intensity. Given that the Lectin channel is also affected by this signal background but does not have any fiber signal, it can be used to remove the sample background signal from the PGP9.5 channel.

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If there is still some overall sample signal in the PGP9.5 channel, try to increase this value to remove it further

docs.title: Correct Epidermis (optional)
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls whether to correct for a strong signal in the upper part of the epidermis.

Why it matters

It can happen that the epidermis shows a strong signal that is almost of the same intensity as the fibers. It is therefore difficult for the algorithm to distinguish between the epidermis and the fibers using threshold-based approaches. With this parameter, the algorithm will try to correct for this signal by segmenting the epidermis and lowering the signal in this region.

How to choose

  • In most cases, it is not necessary to set this parameter to True.
  • If the results are not satisfactory (epidermis segmented with the fibers), try to set this parameter to True

Here is an example of a too strong signal in the epidermis region:

And the improvement using this parameter:

docs.title: Analysis Resolution Level
docs.text:
What it does

This parameter controls which resolution of the OME-Zarr multi-resolution pyramid is used to detect the Dermis–Epidermis junction.

OME-Zarr images are stored as a multi-resolution pyramid:

  • level 0 → full resolution (largest, most detailed)
  • higher levels → downsampled versions (smaller, faster)

Why it matters

Using a lower resolution:

  • uses less memory
  • runs faster
  • is usually sufficient and even better due to intensity averaging to detect the surface shape

Using a very high resolution:

  • increases RAM usage
  • may cause the task to fail on large datasets
  • might be counterproductive, as a slight downsampling reduces noise and improve fiber detection

How to choose

  • In most cases, the default value is sufficient
  • If your image is too small (less than 1G) → keep the default value
  • If your image is large or your computer runs out of memory → increase the resolution level

docs.title: Local Run Command
docs.text: python run_segment_fibers.py

docs.title: Usage Overview
docs.text:
This package provides two ways of running the SkInnervation3D processing tasks depending on the available resources:

  1. Using Fractal Environment Provided that a full Fractal deployment is available, this is the most convenient approach when working with large datasets or when you want to leverage workflow automation and cluster execution. 👉 Fractal Execution

  2. Using local SkInnervation3D App Provided that the SkInnervation3D App is installed, it is the most convenient approach to run workflows on a local machine. The SkInnervation3D app provides a user interface to define and launch the analysis workflows. See the SkInnervation3D App documentation for more information and how to install the application.

  3. Using local Python files In the absence of the Fractal environment, each task can be run locally using python files. This is useful for testing, development, debugging, or processing smaller datasets on a local machine. 👉 Local Execution

All methods use the same underlying task definitions. The main difference lies in how task execution is orchestrated.

docs.title: Using Fractal Environment
docs.text: This section explains how to run the SkInnervation3D tasks within the Fractal platform using workflows.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
A working Fractal installation. If you are new to Fractal, there is a small wiki that describes the first steps to build a workflow:

👉 Fractal Wiki

This guide explains how to:

  • create a project
  • create a dataset
  • assemble workflows
  • add tasks
  • run workflows
  • visualise outputs

docs.title: Selecting SkInnervation3D Tasks
docs.text:
As the skinnervation-fractal-tasks task package will remain private, it will not be available in the list of collections of tasks provided by Fractal under the Tasks Tab by default. The task package needs to be manually collected by providing a wheel file of the current package. This needs normally to be done only once:

  1. Download a wheel file of the package from the github repository (under releases)
  2. On Fractal website, go to the Tasks tab and click on Manage Tasks
  3. Select Local wh for the package type
  4. Provide the path to the wheel file of the skinnervation3d-fractal-tasks package previously downloaded
  5. Select the option private task (optional)
  6. Run Collect

docs.title: Using Local Python Files
docs.text:
All tasks in this package can also be executed outside of Fractal using standard Python files. This is useful for:

  • testing and debugging
  • running tasks locally
  • integrating tasks into custom scripts or pipelines
  • development and verification

Local execution relies on small Python files that act as “task launchers,” each containing all parameters required for a given task.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
Below is the recommended workflow for running tasks locally.

  1. Copy the Python File Templates

    Template files are stored in the repository and kept up to date with each release. Each template includes a complete list of parameters for a specific task.

    To avoid accidentally editing the original files, you should copy the templates before modifying them. This only needs to be done once unless you want to refresh them later.

    cd path/to/skinnervation3d-fractal-tasks
copy-run-templates

    Or for Windows:

    cd C:\path\to\skinnervation3d-fractal-tasks
copy-run-templates

    This will generate editable files in the current directory, following the naming pattern: 

    run_<taskname>.py

  2. Edit Template File

    Before running the task, you need to edit the template file to set the parameters specific to your dataset. This includes the path to the OME-Zarr image to process for example.

  3. Run the task

    Once the template file is edited, you can run the task using the following command:

    python run_<taskname>.py

Tip

In case the list of parameters has been compromised, you can reiterate the copying step to retrieve the latest version of the template files.
